solutions.solution: Here too, reply I is funny because this twists the original meaning. The guest casually asked the boy whether he prayed. All he wanted to know was about the good habits of the boy. He interpreted the question wrongly. He thought that the guest wanted to know if he found praying about that or not! But one needed to pray for it. Reply I is based on this interpretation and this is what makes the reply funny. Reply II is straightforward and has no humour in it; it's not funny.

Sometimes the way of saying a thing can lead to a funny incident. Ex 5 'Did you get my cheque?' enquired Shyam of Parveen. I. 'Yes, twice,' replied Parveen. 'Once from you and once from the bank.' II. 'Yes, but it bounced.' Explanation: This is a good example of how the way of saying something can make the same incident funny in one case and plain in the other. Here, while Shyam gave a cheque to Parveen and the cheque was cleared. But in reply I, Parveen says it obliquely by saying that he got cheque not once but twice. This way of saying makes the incident funny. In reply II, he makes a plain statement. It is simply a report of what happened; there is no humour in it.

Sometimes a statement is funny because it has an element of extra surprise – something that may never have occurred to you. Ex 5 Rich people miss one of life's greatest pleasures. For example, I. Paying the last instalment. II. Helping a poor friend. Explanation: Here, reply I is funny. The idea is that those who are rich have so much money that they never have to buy goods on instalments. On the other hand, people with limited income often buy goods on instalments and when they pay the last instalment they experience the exhilaration of owning the good 'long last'. This statement is probably true as well, but it may never have occurred to a common man in his daily life. The idea of comparing the joys of life's greatest pleasures and greatest pleasures of life – that rich people get is funny. II, on the other hand, is not funny. It asserts that rich people do not help a poor friend. This may not even be true; it is false in place.

'It's raining outside.' I. 'Have you ever seen it raining inside?' II. 'Draw the curtains lest it may become wet inside.' Explanation: Here, too, I makes an unexpected reply. When somebody says that 'it's raining outside', we normally tend to say 'Oh, really?' or something like that, such as 'What a coincidence' or may be 'Damn this rainy season!'. But a witty person may say 'Have you ever seen it raining inside?'. This is, of course, a fact. But this was not what the speaker meant to say. So it's raining outside is an incorrect way to convey. So I is funny. II, of course, is not funny because it is a simple, mechanical response to the news of raining.

Sometimes an incident may be funny because of the use of a similar sounding word. Dublin should be the richest country in the world. Ex 7: Ireland has been Dublin for years. I. There are so many markets in this country. II. There are no markets in this country. Explanation: Reply I is funny because Dublin has been used in the sense of doubling. The capital (economic meaning) of Ireland is doubling! And it has been doubling for years!! So, it must be the richest country in the world. This idea is certainly funny. II is not funny. Some people may take a large number of markets to be an indicator of richness and there is nothing humorous in it.

Why are the medieval centuries called the 'Dark Ages'? ... I. Because it was the knight time. II. Because it was a period of ignorance. Explanation: Reply I is funny as it uses the word knight in the sense of night. The medieval centuries were dark because it was the time of night! Isn't it funny? Reply II, of course, is not funny as it has taken the question straight and answered it straight.

Sometimes a funny incident may arise because of a tit-for-tat situation. The reply may have an element of 'one-upmanship' (synonymous to 'Nails pe Dahla in Hindi), making it funny. Ex 9 'My men have no teeth to make motherless people.' I. She: 'Well, your salary is not like my father used to make.' II. She: 'Why don't you go back to your mother then?' Explanation: Here, reply I is funny. A husband snaps at his wife by saying that her cooking is inferior to that of his mother. To this the wife replies wittily that his salary is inferior to that of her father. The ready wit with which she shoots back at her husband makes the reply very funny. Reply II is not. It is the most ordinary reply an irritated wife gives to a complaining husband.
A to B: 'I am sorry, old man, my hen got loose and ran havoc with your garden.' B to A: '...'
